companyCaseStudy.title: How Uber Used H3 to Index Billions of Location Events — Geo-Queries 10x Faster and Dispatch Throughput Up 5x
companyCaseStudy.summary: Uber was ingesting billions of GPS pings and making millions of dispatch and pricing geospatial queries per minute. They built and open-sourced H3 — a hexagonal hierarchical spatial index — cutting median geo-lookup latency from ~120ms to single-digit ms, increasing matching throughput ~5x and letting teams reason about space with a compact integer index.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

Location is central to Uber: pricing, matching, heatmaps, fraud detection — all need fast, scalable geospatial queries. At scale that means billions of events per day and millions of queries per minute. Traditional geometry queries (point-in-polygon, tile joins) were painful and slow. Uber invented H3 (an open-source hexagonal hierarchical spatial index) to turn 2D geographic problems into cheap integer operations and neighborhood lookups.

## The Problem

- Scale: billions of location pings/day from riders and drivers.
- Performance: geospatial lookups (nearest cells, local aggregations, geofencing) were taking tens to hundreds of milliseconds using expensive spherical geometry and database spatial indexes.
- Complexity: different services used different representations (lat/lon, tiles, polygons), making cross-service joins and analytics hard.

The result: slow dispatch decisions, costly aggregation pipelines, and brittle logic repeated across teams.

## Challenges

- Accuracy vs performance: preserve enough geographic fidelity for pricing and matching while enabling cheap lookup operations.
- Hierarchy and neighborhoods: need fast expansion of “nearby” cells at different zoom/resolution levels for radius queries and heatmaps.
- Integration: adopt a single index across microservices, analytics, and streaming pipelines without massive migration overhead.

## Solution — H3 (Hexagonal Hierarchical Spatial Index)

Uber built H3, a system that:

- Maps lat/lon to compact 64-bit integers (H3 indexes) at discrete hierarchical resolutions.
- Uses hexagons (instead of square tiles) to reduce distortion and simplify neighborhood math.
- Provides efficient APIs for k-ring (neighbors), polygon fill, and hierarchical aggregation.

Architectural decisions:

- Represent spatial location everywhere as an H3 index at an appropriate resolution rather than raw lat/lon for service-to-service contracts.
- Do neighborhood queries with k-ring (O(k) integer ops) instead of repeated spatial queries against a DB.
- Precompute polygon->H3 cell sets (polyfills) for geofences and store them in a small, fast key-value cache (memcached/Redis) to allow constant-time membership checks.
- Use different resolutions for different use-cases: coarse for analytics heatmaps, finer for dispatch and pricing.

### How it fits in the stack

```mermaid
graph LR
  MobileClient -->|GPS pings| Ingest[Event Ingest]
  Ingest --> Stream[Kafka/Stream]
  Stream --> Dispatch[Dispatch Service]
  Stream --> Analytics[Aggregation Pipeline]
  Dispatch --> H3Service[H3 Index Service / Library]
  Analytics --> H3Service
  H3Service --> KV[Redis / Memcached]
  H3Service --> DB[Postgres / MySQL]
  Dispatch --> MatchEngine[Matching Engine]
  MatchEngine --> Drivers
```

- GPS pings are converted to H3 indexes at ingest and pushed into streaming.
- Dispatch queries the H3 APIs for k-rings and membership checks, then feeds the candidate drivers into the matching engine.
- Analytics pipelines use H3 polyfills to produce fast, compact heatmaps.

## Before vs After (Representative metrics)

| Metric | Before (geometry DB / raw lat/lon) | After (H3 index + k-ring + cache) |
|--------|------------------------------------:|-----------------------------------:|
| Median geo-lookup latency (per query) | ~120 ms | ~8 ms |
| Dispatch candidate expansion throughput | 1x | ~5x |
| Server CPU on geo workloads | ~70% | ~30% |
| Storage for geofence representation | Huge polygons (KBs per fence) | Compact H3 cell sets (tens to hundreds of bytes) |
| Developer integration / cognitive load | Many bespoke shapes & functions | Single integer index & small API |

> Note: numbers are representative of the improvements teams reported when moving from geometry-heavy queries to H3-backed flows at scale.

## Why hexes? (Short analogy)

Think of mapping Earth's surface to puzzle pieces. Squares line up on a flat map but distort a sphere; triangles are messy; hexagons tile uniformly and keep neighbors consistent — like moving from a pixelated map to smooth honeycomb cells that are easy to reason about.

## Concrete wins

- Latency: Median geospatial lookup and membership checks moved from ~100ms+ (database spatial operations and point-in-polygon checks) down to single-digit milliseconds using in-process integer ops and cached polyfills.
- Throughput: Dispatch services could evaluate many more candidate drivers per request (k-ring expansions are cheap), increasing match quality and reducing wait time.
- Simplicity: Teams standardized on H3 indexes in events and APIs; analytics, fraud, and mapping teams could share the same cell sets.
- Open source: Publishing H3 (https://github.com/uber/h3) amplified community contributions and made it easy to reuse the index outside Uber.

## Practical pattern used at Uber

1. Normalize at ingest: compute an H3 index for every GPS ping and store it alongside lat/lon.
2. Precompute polyfills for static geofences at appropriate resolutions and cache them.
3. Use k-ring expansions for radius/neighbor queries instead of range queries in DB.
4. Choose resolution per use-case: lower resolution for analytics, higher for dispatch.
5. Fall back to exact geometry only when strictly necessary (e.g., regulatory boundaries).

## Risks and trade-offs

- Lossy representation: H3 discretizes the earth; very small features < cell size can be lost. Mitigation: pick resolution carefully and fall back to polygon checks for edge cases.
- Migration surface: switching to H3 requires updating many services to accept integer indexes; Uber did this incrementally by adding H3 alongside lat/lon and switching consumers over time.

## Results (summary)

- Faster lookup latencies (single-digit ms vs ~120ms), higher dispatch throughput (~5x candidate expansion), and smaller, more consistent geofence representations.
- A single, shareable spatial primitive reduced duplicated geospatial logic across teams and simplified analytics.

## Visual: neighborhood lookup (k-ring)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Cell x] --> B[k=1 neighbors]
  B --> C[k=2 neighbors]
  C --> D[Aggregate candidates]
  D --> E[Matching Engine]
```

## When to use this approach

- You have massive volumes of location events (millions per minute / billions per day).
- You need fast neighborhood queries, heatmaps, or membership checks.
- You can tolerate discretization at a chosen resolution.
keyTakeaways: Turn geometry into integers: indexing lat/lon to compact H3 cells reduces geo-ops to cheap integer and set operations (median geo-lookup latency went from ~120ms to single-digit ms in representative flows).
Use hierarchical resolutions: pick coarse cells for analytics and finer cells for matching; k-ring gives O(k) neighbor expansion vs expensive spatial joins.
Cache polyfills for static shapes and migrate incrementally: keep lat/lon until all consumers accept H3 to reduce rollout risk.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
from h3 import h3

# Get H3 index for a lat/lon at resolution 9
lat, lon = 37.775938728915946, -122.41795063018799
index = h3.geo_to_h3(lat, lon, 9)
print('H3 index:', index)

# Get the k-ring neighbors (radius = 2)
neighbors = h3.k_ring(index, 2)
print('Neighbors:', neighbors)

# Fill a polygon with H3 cells at resolution 8
polygon = {"type": "Polygon", "coordinates": [[[ -122.42, 37.77 ], [ -122.41, 37.77 ], [ -122.41, 37.78 ], [ -122.42, 37.78 ], [ -122.42, 37.77 ]]]}
cell_set = h3.polyfill(polygon['coordinates'][0], 8)
print('Polyfilled cell count:', len(cell_set))

concept.title: Distributed Locks & Leases: Safely Coordinating Resources Across Machines
concept.summary: Distributed locks (and leases) let multiple processes coordinate access to shared resources across machines—so only one worker acts on a resource at a time. They solve race conditions, duplicate work, and inconsistent state in distributed systems, but must be used with care to avoid deadlocks, single points of failure, and subtle timing bugs.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

Imagine a fleet of workers picking up tasks from a queue. If two workers pick the same task and both process it, you get duplicate work or inconsistent writes. In a single machine you grab a mutex; in a distributed system you need a *distributed lock* or *lease*.

**Analogy:** a parking spot with a physical key. You use the key to claim the spot. If you lose the key or don't return it, others can't park. A *lease* improves this by giving the spot for a limited time — when the timer expires the spot is free again even if the key is lost.

## What is a distributed lock vs a lease?
- **Distributed lock:** a mechanism that provides mutual exclusion across nodes — only one client may hold the lock at a time. Locks usually require an explicit release.
- **Lease:** a lock with a time-to-live (TTL). If the holder crashes and doesn't release, the lease expires and others can acquire it. Leases are safer in unreliable networks.

When to use: leader election, single-writer operations (migrations, cron jobs), coordinating access to non-transactional resources (external APIs, file systems), and scheduling where exactly-one semantics are required.

When not to use: high-frequency short operations where optimistic concurrency or idempotency works better. Locks add latency and can become bottlenecks.

---

## How it works (approaches)

1. **Centralized store (Redis, relational DB):** Clients perform atomic operations (SET NX, INSERT) to claim a lock token. Simpler but the store can be a single point of failure.
2. **Consensus-backed (etcd, Zookeeper):** Built-in primitives for locks/leases and leader election. Stronger safety across failures, but more complex.
3. **Algorithms (Redlock, leases + tokens):** Patterns around Redis to make locking robust, or use library implementations.

### Mermaid: Basic flowchart (client trying to acquire a lease)
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Client[Client] -->|Try acquire| Store{Lock Store}
  Store -->|Grant + token| Client
  Client -->|Work inside TTL| Resource[Resource]
  Client -->|Release| Store
  Store -->|Expired / Released| OtherClients(Other Clients)
```

### Mermaid: Sequence: Client <-> Redis (SET NX PX)
```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>Redis: SET lock_key token NX PX 30000
  Redis-->>Client: OK (or nil)
  alt OK
    Client->>Resource: perform operation
    Client->>Redis: EVAL(check-and-del script)
  else nil
    Client->>Client: retry/backoff
  end
```

---

## BEFORE / AFTER: naive vs lock/lease

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| No lock (naive) | Simple, low latency | Duplicate work, race conditions | Idempotent tasks only
| Database row lock (SELECT FOR UPDATE) | Uses DB transactions, strong safety | Ties to DB; long locks hurt throughput | DB-bound critical sections
| Redis SET NX + TTL (lease) | Fast, distributed, TTL prevents deadlock | Needs safe release, clock/TTL tuning | Short critical sections, fast coordination
| Consensus-backed lock (etcd/ZK) | Strong correctness across failures | Operational complexity, slower | Leader election, critical coordination

---

## Practical code examples and patterns

Key principles to implement safely:
- Use an *owner token* returned when acquiring. Only the owner may release the lock.
- Prefer **leases (TTL)** so locks auto-expire if the holder dies.
- Use atomic release (check token then delete) — via Lua script in Redis or transactional operations.
- Renew the lease only if you still hold it and you need more time (keep-alive). If keep-alive fails, stop working.

### Example: Redis SET NX PX + safe release (conceptual Python)
```python
# Acquire
token = uuid4().hex
ok = redis.set('lock:order:123', token, nx=True, px=30000)
if ok:
    try:
        do_critical_work()
    finally:
        # safe release using Lua script (check token then del)
        script = """
        if redis.call('get', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
          return redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        else
          return 0
        end
        """
        redis.eval(script, 1, 'lock:order:123', token)
else:
    # retry with backoff or abort
```

### Example: etcd lease + lock (Go-esque)
- Create a lease with TTL
- Attach lease to a key (create key under /locks/name with lease)
- If client dies and doesn't renew the lease, lease expires and key is removed

These libraries (etcd/clientv3, kazoo for ZK) implement safe lock primitives — prefer them over raw primitives when possible.

---

## Common pitfalls (and how to avoid them)

- **Assuming TTL makes you safe without renewals:** If your work can exceed TTL, you must renew the lease atomically; otherwise another client can acquire and both will work simultaneously.
- **Clock skew confusion:** Don’t rely on synchronized clocks for safety; use TTLs and server-side expirations rather than client-local timers for correctness.
- **Wrong release (deleted by wrong process):** Always use owner tokens and atomic compare-and-delete when releasing.
- **Overusing locks:** Locks reduce concurrency and can be a bottleneck. Prefer idempotency, optimistic retries, or partitioning to reduce need for locks.
- **Single point of failure:** If you use a single Redis without replication, its outage or split-brain can break correctness. Use replicated/consensus-backed stores for critical locks.
- **Long critical sections:** Hold locks for the minimal time. If you must do long work, consider splitting the job or using work-stealing patterns.

---

## Best practices
- Use **short TTL leases** and renew only if needed; fail-fast if renewal fails.
- Store an **opaque owner token** and use atomic release scripts.
- Prefer **consensus-backed** locks (etcd, ZK) for critical coordination when you need strong safety under partitions.
- Use **leader election** primitives when your intent is a single active coordinator, rather than ad-hoc locking.
- Consider **optimistic concurrency** (compare-and-swap) instead of locks where possible.

---

## Conclusion
Distributed locks and leases are powerful tools for coordinating resource access across machines, but they introduce complexity: TTL tuning, correct release, retries, and dependency on the lock store. Use them deliberately, prefer library/consensus primitives for correctness, and always design for failure (ownership token + TTL + atomic release).
keyTakeaways: Use leases (TTL) + owner tokens + atomic release to avoid deadlocks and wrong releases.
Prefer consensus-backed locks (etcd/Zookeeper) for critical coordination; Redis is OK for short-lived, high-performance locks if used carefully.
Avoid overusing locks — consider idempotency, optimistic concurrency, partitioning, or leader election instead.
concept.codeExample:
## Safe Redis lock release Lua script (string)

-- This Lua script atomically checks the token and deletes the key only if it matches
if redis.call('get', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
  return redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
else
  return 0
end

# Usage (Python redis-py):
# redis.eval(lua_script, 1, 'lock:order:123', token)
